intro:
Manasseh (meaning who makes to forget or forgetfulness) is the name of two major biblical figures. (1.) The elder son of Joseph and Asenath, born in Egypt before the famine (Genesis 41:51). His name commemorated God's making Joseph forget his toil and his father's house. When Jacob blessed Joseph's sons in Genesis 48, he deliberately crossed his hands to give the greater blessing to the younger Ephraim over the elder Manasseh, establishing the precedence of Ephraim's tribe. Manasseh's tribe settled on both sides of the Jordan — half east of the Jordan in Gilead and half west, making it unique among the tribes.

(2.) The son and successor of Hezekiah as king of Judah (696–642 BC), regarded in the books of Kings as the most wicked of Judah's kings: he reversed Hezekiah's reforms, rebuilt the high places, erected altars to Baal, set up Asherah poles, practiced human sacrifice (burning his sons in the fire), and filled Jerusalem with innocent blood (2 Kings 21:1–18). The Chronicler adds that Manasseh was captured by the Assyrians and taken to Babylon, where he repented and was restored to his throne (2 Chronicles 33:11–17). The book of Kings offers no such account of repentance, creating a significant interpretive tension between the two biblical portraits of this king.
